notes: The vast majority of fish health problems trace back to water quality. Before any other treatment, test the water (ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH, temperature). A water change is often the single most effective immediate intervention.

symptoms.description: Small white spots that look like grains of salt or sugar scattered across the body, fins, or gills. Caused by the parasite Ichthyophthirius multifiliis, one of the most common fish diseases.

possible_causes.brief: Parasitic infection triggered by stress, new fish, or temperature drops
Looks similar but spots are slightly fuzzier
Larger pearly nodules rather than fine spots

home_care: Test water immediately - ammonia and nitrite must be 0, nitrate under 40ppm
Slowly raise tank temperature by 2-3°C over 24 hours (within species tolerance) to speed parasite life cycle
Perform a 25% water change, gravel vacuum thoroughly
Treat with a proprietary ich treatment from an aquatic shop, following dose instructions
Remove activated carbon from the filter during treatment (it removes medication)
Continue treatment for the full course even after spots vanish - parasites in gravel can re-infect

vet_urgency.low: A few spots, fish still active and eating - treat with shop-bought ich medication immediately
vet_urgency.medium: Spots spreading rapidly, fish becoming lethargic - treat plus daily water changes, consider aquatic vet advice
vet_urgency.high: Heavy infestation, fish gasping or clamping fins, multiple species affected - urgent aquatic vet or specialist advice

red_flags: Spots covering most of the body
Fish gasping at the surface (gill infestation)
New fish recently added without quarantine

combinations.concern: Advanced ich, gill involvement - urgent treatment
Significant stress and disease load - intensive treatment and water management

symptoms.description: Fins appear ragged, frayed, or shortened, often with white or black edges. Tissue progressively dies and may extend back to the body if untreated.

possible_causes.brief: Initial damage allows infection to take hold
Ammonia, nitrite, or high nitrate weakens the immune system

home_care: Test water - any ammonia or nitrite means an urgent 50% water change and daily checks
Improve water quality with frequent partial water changes (25% twice weekly)
Check tank mates for aggression - remove fin-nippers if present
Treat with a fin rot medication or broad-spectrum antibacterial from an aquatic shop
Add aquarium salt if the species tolerates it (NOT for catfish, scaleless fish, or live plants)
Ensure the filter is mature and not over-cleaned

vet_urgency.low: Slight fin fraying, otherwise healthy fish - water change and improve husbandry
vet_urgency.medium: Visibly ragged fins, possible discolouration at edges - over-the-counter treatment plus water management
vet_urgency.high: Rot extending to the body, fish lethargic, multiple fish affected - aquatic vet or specialist needed

red_flags: Rot reaching the body / base of fins
Red or bloody edges to fins

combinations.concern: Combined bacterial and fungal infection - treat both

symptoms.description: Fish struggles to maintain a normal upright position - floats at the surface, sinks to the bottom, swims at an odd angle, or rolls onto its side. Common in fancy goldfish and bettas.

possible_causes.brief: Gas-producing food, especially dry flakes, presses on the swim bladder
Common in fancy goldfish and bettas
Common in selectively bred fancy goldfish

home_care: Skip feeding for 48-72 hours to allow constipation to clear
Offer one cooked, deshelled pea (soft inside) - a mild laxative for fish
Soak dry food before feeding to reduce air ingestion
Switch to sinking pellets if the fish swallows air at the surface
Check water temperature - sudden cold can trigger buoyancy issues
Lower water level if the fish struggles to reach the surface for air

vet_urgency.low: Brief floating after a meal, corrects itself - reduce feeding and offer pea
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent buoyancy issues for several days - try fasting and pea, then bacterial swim bladder treatment
vet_urgency.high: Fish stuck on side, unable to swim, refusing food, getting weaker - aquatic vet for assessment

red_flags: Permanently stuck on side or upside down
Cannot reach food at the surface
Sores developing on exposed skin (from rubbing on substrate)
Refusing food for more than 4-5 days
Other fish bullying the affected fish

combinations.concern: Possible internal organ disease or severe constipation

symptoms.description: Fish stays near the surface gulping water, even when there is no food. Almost always indicates oxygen problems or gill damage. A critical sign requiring immediate action.

possible_causes.brief: Overcrowded tank, no aeration, high temperature
Affect gas exchange in the gills

home_care: Test water immediately - any ammonia/nitrite means a 50% water change now
Increase aeration immediately - add an air stone, increase filter flow, surface agitation
Lower temperature gradually if above species range
Reduce CO2 injection if applicable
Move some fish to a separate tank if overcrowded
Do not feed for 24-48 hours - reduces ammonia production

vet_urgency.low: Not applicable - all surface gasping is urgent
vet_urgency.medium: Brief gasping after feeding or in early morning - test water, increase aeration
vet_urgency.high: Multiple fish gasping at surface, lethargy, deaths - EMERGENCY water change and aeration, then specialist advice

red_flags: Red or pale gills (visible when looking at fish from side)
Recent tank changes (new fish, large water change, filter clean)

combinations.concern: Severe gill stress or damage - emergency

symptoms.description: Fish appears noticeably duller or paler than normal. Colour changes are one of the earliest signs of stress or illness in fish.

possible_causes.brief: New tank, transport, bullying, water changes
Chronic exposure to ammonia/nitrite dulls colour
Older fish naturally lose some intensity
Often the first sign of underlying disease
Some species fade naturally at night

home_care: Test water quality - even slight ammonia or nitrite causes chronic stress
Check for bullying or aggression from tank mates
Improve diet with varied, quality foods including colour-enhancing options
Reduce stress: stable temperature, regular maintenance, hiding places
Photograph weekly to track colour changes objectively
Note time of day - some species are paler in the morning or at night

vet_urgency.low: Slight fade, no other symptoms - improve husbandry and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Noticeable pale appearance with mild lethargy or hiding - water test and intervention
vet_urgency.high: Very pale plus other symptoms (gasping, fin clamping, spots) - treat underlying cause and consider aquatic vet

red_flags: Combined with hiding or appetite loss
White patches (different from overall paling - may be fungus)
Multiple fish becoming pale at the same time

combinations.concern: Significant stress or illness - investigate water and tank mates
Likely ich or parasitic infection - treat

symptoms.description: Fish holds its fins tightly against the body rather than fanned out and relaxed. A general distress sign indicating discomfort.

possible_causes.brief: Recent water change with mismatched pH

home_care: Test water - check ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH, temperature
Perform a 25% water change with dechlorinated water at the same temperature
Check temperature is correct for the species
Look closely for parasites, spots, or fin damage
Reduce stressors: dim lights, quiet area, hiding spots
Do not add new fish or change conditions further while diagnosing

vet_urgency.low: Brief clamping after a water change, returns to normal - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent clamping with mild appetite loss - water management and treat any visible parasites
vet_urgency.high: Clamped fins with gasping, hiding, multiple fish affected - intensive water management and possibly specialist advice

red_flags: Combined with gasping or rapid gill movement

combinations.concern: Water quality crisis - emergency change and aeration

symptoms.description: Body swells dramatically and scales lift outward giving a pinecone appearance when viewed from above. This is a symptom (not a disease) indicating severe internal illness, usually advanced.

possible_causes.brief: Often Aeromonas - causes organ failure and fluid retention

home_care: Isolate the fish in a quarantine tank with matched water parameters
Add aquarium salt (1 tsp per 5L) for species that tolerate it - helps reduce fluid retention
Treat with a broad-spectrum bacterial medication from an aquatic shop
Keep water pristine - daily small water changes
Offer easy-to-eat food but do not overfeed
Be aware: full dropsy has a poor prognosis - seek aquatic vet advice

vet_urgency.medium: Early bloating without scale lifting - antibacterial treatment and intensive water management
vet_urgency.high: Full pinecone scales, lethargy, poor prognosis - aquatic vet consultation, prepare to discuss humane endpoint

red_flags: Multiple fish in tank showing signs

symptoms.description: Fish ignores food, spits it out, or eats noticeably less. Healthy fish should respond promptly at feeding time.

possible_causes.condition: Stress (new tank, new fish, water change)

possible_causes.brief: Cold-blooded fish digest poorly at wrong temperatures
Some fish hide and miss food

home_care: Test water immediately - any ammonia/nitrite is a likely cause
Check temperature is in the correct range for the species
Offer favourite foods or live/frozen alternatives
Observe feeding to check whether bullying is preventing access
Reduce feeding for a few days if recently overfed
Quarantine if suspected disease - allows direct feeding without competition

vet_urgency.low: Skipped one meal but otherwise active - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Not eating for 3-5 days, mild lethargy - water management and dietary changes
vet_urgency.high: Not eating for over a week, weight loss, other symptoms - aquatic vet or specialist

red_flags: Combined with weight loss (visible sunken body)
Other illness signs (spots, fin damage, clamping)

combinations.concern: Underlying illness - investigate water and disease
Constipation or internal disease - treat with pea/medication

symptoms.description: Fish hides much more than normal, spends most time motionless, or lies on the bottom. Most fish should be active during the day (with species-specific exceptions like nocturnal catfish).

possible_causes.condition: Stress (new fish, new tank, water change)

possible_causes.brief: Sick fish withdraw from the group

home_care: Observe tank dynamics quietly - is bullying happening?
Test water for ammonia, nitrite, nitrate, pH, temperature
Check for visible illness signs (spots, fin damage, colour change)
Provide more hiding spots so timid fish feel secure
Consider rehoming aggressive tank mates
If new arrival - allow 1-2 weeks to settle before worrying

vet_urgency.low: Brief hiding after a disturbance, otherwise normal - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent hiding for several days, eating reduced - investigate water and tank dynamics
vet_urgency.high: Lying on bottom, gasping, unresponsive - serious illness, urgent intervention

red_flags: Lying on the bottom on its side
Other fish in the tank also affected

symptoms.description: Gills (the flaps just behind the eyes) move much faster than normal. Healthy fish breathe steadily; rapid movement signals respiratory or water quality stress.

possible_causes.brief: Tank too warm or undersaturated with air
Damages gills and forces compensatory breathing
Brief increase after eating is normal

home_care: Test water immediately - check ammonia, nitrite, temperature
Increase aeration (air stone, surface movement) immediately
Reduce temperature gradually if too warm
Perform 25-50% water change with dechlorinated, temperature-matched water
Look at gills - are they red, swollen, pale, or producing extra mucus?
Quarantine if parasites suspected and treat appropriately

vet_urgency.low: Brief increase after feeding, returns to normal - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Sustained rapid breathing with mild lethargy - water management and parasite check
vet_urgency.high: Rapid breathing plus gasping at surface, multiple fish affected, deaths - EMERGENCY

combinations.concern: Severe oxygen or toxin crisis - emergency

symptoms.description: Eyes appear cloudy white, hazy, or bulging outward (pop-eye / exophthalmia). Can affect one or both eyes and may indicate local injury or systemic illness.

possible_causes.brief: Most common single cause of cloudy eyes
Both eyes bulging suggests internal disease

home_care: Test water and perform a 25% water change with dechlorinated water
Check decor for sharp edges if one eye affected - smooth or remove
Add aquarium salt for species that tolerate it
Treat with antibacterial medication if infection suspected
Improve diet variety and quality
Isolate affected fish to monitor and reduce competition for food

vet_urgency.low: Slight cloudiness, one eye, clear injury cause - improve husbandry and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Cloudy eye persisting beyond a few days, mild bulging - antibacterial treatment and water management
vet_urgency.high: Both eyes bulging dramatically, combined with bloating or lethargy - urgent treatment, consider aquatic vet

red_flags: Severe bulging (eye looks ready to detach)

symptoms.description: White, grey, or off-white cotton-wool-like growths on skin, fins, or mouth. Usually a secondary infection following injury or poor water quality.

possible_causes.brief: Most common aquarium fungus, secondary to injury or stress
Looks fungal but is bacterial - needs antibacterial treatment
Develops where skin has been damaged

home_care: Test water and perform a 25% water change
Identify and remove the underlying cause (sharp decor, bullying, poor parameters)
Treat with antifungal medication from an aquatic shop
If mouth/lips affected, treat as Columnaris (bacterial) too
Add aquarium salt for species that tolerate it (helps recovery)
Quarantine if multiple fish unaffected - reduces spread risk

vet_urgency.low: Small isolated patch on a fin, fish otherwise well - shop-bought antifungal and water management
vet_urgency.medium: Spreading patches, multiple sites - combined antifungal/antibacterial treatment
vet_urgency.high: Extensive fungus, mouth or gill involvement, lethargy - urgent treatment and possibly specialist input

red_flags: Mouth or jaw fungus (often Columnaris, fast-progressing)
